meta.source: Microsoft Learn (learn.microsoft.com/entra/identity/authentication) — admin-center paths, controls, and enforcement model grounded via the Microsoft Learn MCP on 2026-07-03.
generalNotes: Manage these in the Microsoft Entra admin center (entra.microsoft.com) with at least the Authentication Policy Administrator role (SSPR properties, registration campaign, auth strengths). Conditional Access / Security Defaults changes need the Conditional Access Administrator role.

Legacy MFA and SSPR method management is retired — since Sept 30 2025 authentication methods live only in Entra ID > Protection > Authentication methods. Configure methods there, not in the old MFA/SSPR service-settings pages.

The Tier0 'Password Protection' row is intentionally not duplicated here: it shares the exact policy name of the Tier1 entry, so it already inherits that guidance (global banned-password list + smart lockout). Verify only — don't add a second key.

requiredSettings.label: How tenant-wide MFA is actually enforced
requiredSettings.value: There is no single 'require MFA for all' toggle in the Authentication methods blade. Enforce it with a Conditional Access policy 'Require MFA for all users' (Business Premium includes Entra ID P1). If you have no P1, use Security Defaults instead — the two are mutually exclusive.

requiredSettings.label: Authentication methods > Settings — System-preferred MFA
requiredSettings.value: Enabled (prompts each user for their most secure registered method)

requiredSettings.label: Authentication methods > Settings — Report suspicious activity
requiredSettings.value: Enabled (lets users flag rogue MFA prompts; feeds Identity Protection)

steps: Confirm the enforcement layer first: in Entra ID > Overview > Properties > Manage security defaults, Security Defaults should be Disabled when you use Conditional Access (leave it Enabled only for a P1-less tenant).

Create/verify the CA policy: Entra ID > Protection > Conditional Access > Policies > New policy — Users = All users, Exclude = break-glass/emergency accounts (and Directory Synchronization Accounts if hybrid); Target resources = All resources; Grant = Require multifactor authentication (or Require authentication strength > Multifactor authentication).
Set the policy to Report-only, review the sign-in impact, then switch it to On.
In Entra ID > Protection > Authentication methods > Settings: set System-preferred MFA = Enabled and Report suspicious activity = Enabled (target All users).

Verify: run a test sign-in for a non-excluded user and confirm an MFA prompt; check Sign-in logs show the CA policy applied. Break-glass accounts must stay excluded and be covered by a separate monitored control.

Note (HIGH impact): every user without a registered method is forced to enroll on next sign-in — stage with communications and a registration campaign, and always exclude emergency-access accounts before switching from Report-only to On.

requiredSettings.label: Control (per the portal path / description)
requiredSettings.value: The MFA Registration campaign — Entra ID > Protection > Authentication methods > Registration campaign — that nudges users to set up Microsoft Authenticator during sign-in.

requiredSettings.label: Days allowed to snooze
requiredSettings.value: 1 (optionally cap total snoozes via enforceRegistrationAfterAllowedSnoozes so setup becomes mandatory)

steps: Entra ID > Protection > Authentication methods > Registration campaign.
Set State = Enabled, Method to nudge = Microsoft Authenticator, and Target = All users (exclude break-glass accounts).
Set Days allowed to snooze = 1; to force setup, enable a limited number of snoozes (enforceRegistrationAfterAllowedSnoozes) rather than unlimited.
Save.
Verify: a targeted user without Authenticator sees the 'Set up Authenticator' nudge after their next MFA event; existing Authenticator users are unaffected.

Note: this row is named 'System-preferred MFA' but its portal path and description configure the registration campaign. System-preferred MFA itself is the separate Authentication methods > Settings toggle (covered under the 'Multifactor authentication' row) that auto-selects each user's strongest method — keep that Enabled too.

requiredSettings.label: Allowed method combinations
requiredSettings.value: Phishing-resistant / strong MFA only: Windows Hello for Business, FIDO2 security key, device-bound Authenticator (phone sign-in), Temporary Access Pass (one-time), Microsoft Authenticator push, software OATH, hardware OATH — per the baseline

requiredSettings.label: Consumed by
requiredSettings.value: Conditional Access policies that require elevated assurance (notably the admin-MFA CA policy) — this definition has no effect on its own

steps: Entra ID > Protection > Authentication methods > Authentication strengths > New authentication strength.
Name it (e.g. 'Inforcer - Authentication'), then tick the allowed method combinations for the baseline (phishing-resistant + strong methods listed above).
Save — it then appears as a Grant control option ('Require authentication strength') in Conditional Access.
Point the admin-MFA CA policy (e.g. 'Core - Allow - MFA for Admins') at this strength and confirm it's On.

Verify: the strength shows Read Only = False (editable) and is referenced by at least one active CA policy; an admin sign-in is challenged for a compliant method. No end-user impact comes from the definition alone — it's delivered through the CA policy that consumes it.

requiredSettings.label: Self-service password reset enabled
requiredSettings.value: All (production) — or a pilot group via Selected. Baseline intent is ENABLED; the row notes the tenant currently has it Disabled.

requiredSettings.label: Number of methods required to reset
requiredSettings.value: 1 or 2 (2 raises assurance; keep methods-to-register at least one higher)

requiredSettings.label: Methods available to users
requiredSettings.value: Microsoft Authenticator (app notification/code), Mobile phone, Email — align with the enabled Authentication methods

requiredSettings.label: Require users to register when signing in
requiredSettings.value: Yes; reconfirm authentication info every 90–180 days

steps: Entra ID > Protection > Password reset > Properties: set 'Self-service password reset enabled' to All (or Selected for a pilot group), then Save.
On the Authentication methods page: set 'Number of methods required to reset' (1 or 2) and tick the methods available to users (Authenticator, Mobile phone, Email).
On the Registration page: set 'Require users to register when signing in' = Yes and reconfirmation to 90–180 days; Save.
Verify: register a non-admin test user at https://aka.ms/ssprsetup, then reset at https://aka.ms/sspr; the account resets without helpdesk involvement. (Admins always require two methods to reset.)

Note (HIGH impact): enabling SSPR is the intended secure self-recovery path alongside required MFA — with it disabled, every lockout becomes a helpdesk ticket. This SSPR capability is distinct from 'SelfServicePassConfig' (which blocks external self-service sign-up to the tenant) — don't conflate them.
